title: Total Utility and Marginal Utility
description: Practice what you've learned about total utility, marginal utility, and the law of diminishing marginal utility in this exercise.
topic_path: Introduction to consumer theory: total utility and marginal utility

assessment_items.question_content:
**How does the law of diminishing marginal utility help explain the law of demand?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When a good's price decreases, consumers will demand less of its substitutes and more of the good in question
When the price of a good increases consumers will have less disposable income thus they'll demand less of the good in question
Since each additional unit of a good provides less additional benefit, consumers will not be willing to pay as much for each additional unit
Since additional units of a good cost more to produce, producer will only supply more if its price increases
When a good's price decreases consumers demand increases because they value additional units more than those consumed before

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is meant by the phrase *diminishing marginal utility*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Each additional unit of consumption yields less additional happiness than the previous unit
Each additional unit of input used yields less additional output
Total happiness increases at a constant rate
Total happiness increases at an increasing rate
Total happiness is always decreasing

assessment_items.question_content:
Melanie still enjoys drinking jasmine green tea, but she receives less and less additional happiness with each tea she consumes.

**What is the term for what the statement above describes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Diminishing marginal utility
Increasing total utility
Diminishing total utility
Diminishing average utility
Increasing average utility

assessment_items.question_content:
**According to the law of diminishing marginal utility, which of the following is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The total satisfaction from consuming more of a good always decreases as consumption increases
The satisfaction of consuming additional units of a good will decrease as consumption of the good increases
When marginal utility decreases, total utility will also decrease
Consuming more of a good will always make a consumer worse off
Total satisfaction from a good increases at an increasing rate as more of the good is consumed

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines the term utility as it is used by economists?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the happiness or benefit a consumer experiences from a good
the usefulness of a resource or factor of production to producers of a good
the extra benefit a consumer experiences when buying a good for less than she was willing to pay
when a market allocates resources in a way that maximizes consumer and producer surplus
when an input can be used efficiently by producers

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes what consumers face when trying to make themselves as best off as possible?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Consumers face constraints and must make optimal decisions based on those constraints
Scarcity exists for producers, but not for consumers
Optimal consumption means spending whatever it takes to maximize utility
Consumers combine inputs such that the marginal product per dollar spent is the same for all inputs
Consumers minimize their total utility

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows the utility Anna gets from drinking cups of coffee in a day.

Cups of coffee | Total Utility
-|-
1 | 5
2 | 9
3| 12
4|14
5|15
6| 15
7| 14

**Which of the following best describes the pattern exhibited by Anna's utility from drinking coffee?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Anna's *marginal utility* diminishes as she drinks more coffee, but her total utility will increase until her marginal utility $=0$.
At first her total utility will be low, since she only has had one coffee.
The second and third cups of coffee will provide Anna with less additional utility than the first cup.

Anna's total utility table is:

Quantity of coffees consumed | Total Utility ($TU$) |
- | - 
$1$ | $5$ 
$2$ | $9$
$3$ | $12$
$4$ | $14$
$5$ | $15$

choices.content: It is increasing at a decreasing rate until it hits a maximum value, and then it begins to decrease.
It is decreasing at an increasing rate.
Her total utility is always increasing.
It is increasing by the same amount every time she increases her consumption.
Her total utility is decreasing at a decreasing rate.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows Casandra's marginal utility, expressed in utils ($u$),  experienced each time she visits Wild Waves Water Park in a year.

Number of park visits | Casandra's marginal utility ($MU$) 
- | - 
$1$ | $55u$
$2$ | $50u$
$3$ | $40u$ 
$4$ | $25u$
$5$ | $12u$ 
$6$ | $2u$
$7$ | $-6u$

**How many times will Casandra visit Wild Waves if she has a pass offering her unlimited visits for free?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] times
hints: Marginal utility is the change in a consumer's happiness from consuming one additional unit of a good
As long as marginal utility is positive, consuming additional units of a good will increase a consumer's total utility
If marginal utility is negative, a consumer will not wish to consume a good no matter how cheap it is

If park visits were free, Casandra would still only visit the part $6$ times, since the $7^{th}$ visit actually reduces her total utility. In other words, makes her less happy than after just $6$ visits.

answers.message:
Casandra's marginal utility from a $7^{th}$ visit to Wild Waves is negative. This means she would be made less happy by a $7^{th}$ visit than if she had stopped after her $6^{th}$ visit. 

Therefore, Casandra will not go a $7{th}$ time even if admission is free.

The $7^{th}$ visit provides Casandra with a negative marginal utility, making her less happy than she would be if she only visits $6$ times. Therefore, Casandra will not visit a $7^{th}$ time in a year.

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows Christine's marginal utility of yogurt in a week. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is Christine's total utility if she consumes four cartons of yogurt in a week?**

Total utility =  utils

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The total utility at a level of consumption is the marginal utilities of all the units consumed up to that level added together

Her marginal utility for each of the four yogurts she consumes are:

$\begin{align}1^{st}\text{ yogurt} &=12\\\\
2^{nd}\text{ yogurt}&=10\\\\
3^{rd}\text{ yogurt}&=8\\\\
4^{th}\text{ yogurt}&=6
\end{align}$
Her total utility is the sum of all marginal utilities.

Her total utility when consuming $4$ yogurts is: 


$\begin{align}TU&= 12+10+8+6 \\\\
&= 36\end{align}$

options.alt: A line graph depicts the marginal utility curve, labeled M U, illustrating the relationship between utility in utils on the Y-axis and quantity, Q, on the X-axis. The vertical axis ranges from negative 2 to 12 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 9 with increments of 1. The M U curve slopes downward from left to right, starting at (1, 12) and ending at (7, negative 2). Data points are marked at each unit of quantity along the curve.
options.caption: Christine's marginal utility for yogurt

assessment_items.question_content:
Enzo is deciding whether to buy a gym membership or to pay each time he visits the gym. The table below shows Enzo's expected total utility from visiting the gym in a month.

# of visits | Total utility ($TU$) (in utils)
- | - |
$1$ | $40$
$2$ | $78$
$3$ | $112$
$4$ | $140$
$5$ | $160$
$6$ | $170$
$7$ | $170$
$8$ | $160$
$9$ | $140$

Assume Enzo decides to purchase an unlimited gym membership that allows him to go as often as he wishes after he has paid his monthly membership fee. 

**What is the maximum number of times Enzo will go to the gym?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If the price of additional units of a good is $\$0$, then a consumer will demand the good up until the marginal utility $=0$.
Marginal utility is the change in total utility.
Enzo will not visit the gym more than $7$ times because beyond that his marginal utility is negative.

choices.content: $7$ times
$5$ times
$8$ times
$9$ times
$2$ times

assessment_items.question_content:
This graph shows a consumer's total utility from eating hamburgers in a single meal. 

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following characteristics of the $TU$ function is explained by the law of diminishing marginal utility?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts the total utility curve, labeled T U, illustrating the relationship between utility in utils on the Y-axis and quantity, Q, on the X-axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 9 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 6 with increments of 1. The T U curve is concave, starting at (0, 0), peaking at (3, 8.5), and declining to (5, 5).

choices.content: the fact that the $TU$ function slopes upward for some of the total utility curve
the fact that $TU$ function's slope starts steep but decreases until the $3^{rd}$ hamburger and then becomes downward sloping
the fact that the $TU$ function starts at $0$
the fact that the $TU$ function is always positive
the fact that the $TU$ depends on the quantity consumed

assessment_items.question_content:
**If a good is free, when will a consumer stop wanting to buy the good?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: When marginal utility is negative
At the quantity where marginal utility is at its maximum
Once the total utility equals zero
Once marginal utility equals total utility
Once the marginal utility equals zero

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows Joey's the total utility from the consumption of pairs of sneakers, expressed in utils ($u$).

# of pairs of sneakers | Joey's total utility (in utils)
- | - 
$1$  | $100$ 
$2$ | $175$ 
$3$ | $215$ 
$4$ | $230$ 
$5$ | $235$ 
$6$ | $235$ 

**How much additional utility will the $4^{\text{th}}$ pair of shoes provide Joey?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The marginal utility of any unit of consumption is the difference between the total utility from that unit of consumption and the previous unit of consumption.
Joey's total utility of the $3^{rd}$ unit was $215$ utils and his utility from the $4^{th}$ unit was $230$ utils.

His marginal utility of the fourth unit is:

$\begin{align}MU_{4^{\text{th}} \text{ unit}} &=TU_{4^{\text{th}}}-TU_{3^{\text{rd}}}\\\\
&=230-215 \\\\
&= 15\end{align}$

choices.content: $15\text{ utils}$
$230 \text{ utils}$
$1 \text{ utils}$
$-15\text{ utils}$
$215 \text{ utils}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows Casandra's marginal utility from visits to SplashTastica Water park, expressed in utils ($u$)

Number of park visits | Casandra's marginal utility ($Mu$) 
- | - 
$1$ | $55$ 
$2$ | $50$ 
$3$ | $40$  
$4$ | $25$ 
$5$ | $12$ 
$6$ | $2$ 
$7$ | $-6$
$8$ | $-20$

**What is Cassandra's total utility if she visits the park 4 times?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We are asked to find total utility, the sum of all of the utility she gets from all four visits.

The first visit provided $55$ units of utility. The second provided $50$ more units of utility. Her third visit provided $40$ additional units beyond the third, and the fourth visit provided yet another $25$ units.
To find the total utility of the fourth visit, you must add all of the marginal utilities from the each of the four visits.

$55+50+40+25 = 170$ 

Cassandra gets $170$ units of utility from four visits.

assessment_items.question_content:
The table below shows Karina's marginal utility she experiences when she visits Wild Waves Water Park.

Number of park visits | Karina's marginal utility ($MU$) 
- | - 
$1$ | $55$ 
$2$ | $50$ 
$3$ | $40$  
$4$ | $25$ 
$5$ | $12$ 
$6$ | $2$ 
$7$ | $-6$
$8$ | $-20$

**How many visits provides Karina the highest total utility?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: We are asked to find the number of visits that provides Karina the highest total utility.
The first six visits provide positive marginal utility. The seventh and eighth visits provide negative marginal utility.

As long as the marginal utility of a visit is positive, that visit add to her total utility. The 6th visit has a positive utility, but if she visits one more time her marginal utility is negative and her total utility starts to decrease.
Therefore, visiting the park six times provides the highest total utility for Karina.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is used to measure a consumer's entire satisfaction or happiness of a choice?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Total utility
Marginal utility
Marginal Cost
Total revenue
Total costs

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the difference between total utility and marginal utility?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Total utility is consumers’ satisfaction with all consumption; marginal utility is consumers’ satisfaction from one more unit of consumption.
Total utility is consumers’ satisfaction of the last unit of consumption; marginal utility is consumers’ satisfaction from all consumption.
Total utility is the dollar value of all consumption; marginal utility is the dollar value of one more unit of consumption
Total utility is the value to sellers of all production; marginal utility is the value to sellers of all production less the costs of production.
Total utility is the value to sellers of all production; marginal utility is the value to sellers of one more unit of production

assessment_items.question_content:
Consuming one topping on your rolled ice-cream yields 50 utils and consuming the second topping increases your satisfaction by 30 utils. 

**What is true based on this information?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The total utility of consuming two toppings is $80$ utils
The marginal utility of the first topping is $30$ utils
The marginal utility of the second topping is $20$ utils
The marginal utility of consuming two toppings is $80$ utils
The total utility of consuming $2$ toppings is less than the total utility of consuming $1$ topping
